text: Carr argued that hyperlinks — the defining feature of web text — are not neutral navigational aids. Each link is a micro-interruption: a decision node that demands a split-second evaluation of whether to continue or divert. Cognitive scientists call this kind of low-level decision-making 'switching cost.' Even when a reader chooses not to follow a link, the act of evaluating it consumes working memory. A 2007 study by researchers at Cornell found that students who browsed the web during a lecture performed significantly worse on comprehension tests — not because they accessed irrelevant content, but because the act of switching attention repeatedly depleted the cognitive resources required for understanding.
question: According to the Cornell study cited in the passage, why did students who browsed the web during lectures perform worse on comprehension tests?

options.text: They accessed content unrelated to the lecture topic
Screen brightness reduced their sustained focus
Repeated attention-switching depleted the cognitive resources needed for understanding
Multitasking overloaded their short-term memory with irrelevant information
